_meta.authoringNotes: Citations verified against NCCN Melanoma Guidelines v3.2024, Swetter SM AAD/JAAD 2019 (PMID 30614340), Higgins HW JAAD 2015 (PMID 26209218), and SEER melanoma data 2024.

h1Title: Melanoma In Situ: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Melanoma in situ is the earliest detectable stage of melanoma — malignant melanocytes confined entirely to the epidermis above the basement membrane, with no invasion of dermis or potential to metastasize. It accounts for roughly 30% of new melanoma diagnoses in the United States (about 30,000 cases annually, SEER 2024) and incidence has risen 3-4% per year over two decades, driven mainly by ultraviolet exposure and improved dermoscopy-aided detection. Most lesions arise on chronically sun-damaged skin of older adults, classically as a slowly enlarging, irregularly pigmented macule on the face (the lentigo maligna subtype) or as a flat asymmetric pigmented lesion on the trunk or limbs. With complete surgical excision at 5-10 mm margins, the 5-year disease-specific survival exceeds 99% and the risk of progression to invasive melanoma is essentially eliminated.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 30,000 new US cases annually (SEER 2024); 30% of all new melanoma diagnoses
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly fair-skinned adults; median age 65 years; men slightly more affected than women
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 65; lentigo maligna subtype typically over 70
keyStats.globalCases: Incidence highest in Australia, New Zealand, Norway, and the US

definition: Melanoma in situ (ICD-10: D03) is malignant proliferation of melanocytes restricted to the epidermis, above the basement membrane. Because malignant cells have not crossed into the dermis, there is no access to lymphatics or blood vessels and therefore no biological capacity to metastasize. The American Joint Committee on Cancer classifies melanoma in situ as Stage 0 (Tis, N0, M0) under the AJCC 8th edition. Four major histologic subtypes are recognized: lentigo maligna (on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck of older adults), superficial spreading in situ (the most common subtype, on intermittently sun-exposed skin of trunk and limbs), acral lentiginous in situ (on palms, soles, and nail units), and mucosal in situ (rare, on conjunctiva, oral, genital, or anorectal mucosa). Recurrence rates depend on subtype and excision margin: lentigo maligna recurs in 6-20% of conventionally excised cases versus 1-2% with Mohs micrographic surgery. The disease is diagnosed and managed by dermatologists, dermatopathologists, and surgical or Mohs oncologists, with multidisciplinary cancer-center input for cosmetically challenging lentigo maligna of the face.

typesClassification.description: Slowly enlarging irregularly pigmented macule on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck of older adults. Accounts for 35-40% of in situ cases. Often present for years before diagnosis.
Flat asymmetric pigmented lesion on intermittently sun-exposed skin of the trunk, back, and limbs. Most common subtype overall; presents in adults age 40-65.
Arises on palms, soles, or nail units. Disproportionately affects people of color; longitudinal melanonychia with Hutchinson sign warrants biopsy.
Rare; arises on conjunctiva, oral cavity, nasal cavity, genital, or anorectal mucosa. Diagnosis is often delayed and surgical margins challenging.
Lacks visible pigment; presents as a pink or skin-colored macule that mimics actinic keratosis or basal cell carcinoma. Easy to overlook clinically.

primarySymptoms: A pigmented skin lesion that has changed in size, shape, color, or surface texture over months to years — the most common presenting feature.
Asymmetry of a pigmented lesion when divided through the center along any axis — one of the ABCDE warning criteria.
Irregular, notched, or scalloped border that is poorly defined against surrounding skin.
Two or more colors within a single lesion (brown, black, tan, red, pink, white, or blue), often with a streaky or speckled pattern.
Diameter greater than 6 mm, although in situ lesions can also be smaller — relative growth matters more than absolute size.
Evolution over weeks to months — new appearance in adulthood, change in shape, or new symptoms such as itching, tenderness, or bleeding.
A new pigmented streak under a nail (longitudinal melanonychia) wider than 3 mm or extending onto adjacent nail fold skin (Hutchinson sign) — concerning for acral or subungual disease.
A persistent flat or barely raised dark macule on the cheek, temple, or forehead of an older adult — classic lentigo maligna distribution.
An 'ugly duckling' mole — a single lesion that visually differs from the patient's other pigmented lesions.
earlyWarningSigns: Any new pigmented lesion appearing in adulthood, especially after age 40
An existing mole that has begun to change in color, shape, or size
Itching, tenderness, scaling, or bleeding in a pigmented lesion without explanation
Personal history of atypical (dysplastic) nevi, especially in patients with strong family history of melanoma
Childhood or adolescent severe blistering sunburns or extensive use of tanning beds before age 30
emergencySigns: Sudden bleeding from a pigmented lesion without trauma — biopsy needed promptly to exclude invasive disease
Rapid change (within weeks) in size or color of any pigmented lesion — urgent dermatology referral
Ulceration of a pigmented lesion — strongly suggests invasion and requires same-week assessment
New pigmented streak under a nail extending onto the adjacent skin (Hutchinson sign) — biopsy urgently
Palpable lymph node near a known or suspected melanoma site — exclude regional metastasis, although unusual in true in situ disease

causes.description: Cumulative and intermittent UV exposure damages melanocyte DNA, generating signature C-to-T mutations at dipyrimidine sites and activating BRAF, NRAS, and other oncogenes. Severe blistering sunburns in childhood and adolescence are particularly strongly linked to later melanoma risk.

Indoor tanning emits high-intensity UVA and UVB radiation. Any use before age 35 increases melanoma risk by roughly 75% in pooled meta-analyses. The IARC has classified tanning devices as Group 1 carcinogens.

Germline CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1, POT1, and MITF E318K variants confer high lifetime melanoma risk. Patients with two or more first-degree relatives with melanoma have a 35-70% lifetime risk depending on the variant.

causes.cause: Phenotypic susceptibility (fair skin, light eyes, red hair)
causes.description: Fitzpatrick skin types I-II (always or usually burn, rarely tan), red or blond hair, blue or green eyes, and abundant freckling all reflect lower constitutive melanin protection. MC1R variants modulate this phenotypic risk.

Solid organ transplant recipients, hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients, and patients on long-term immunosuppression have a 3-4 fold increased melanoma risk. Mechanism involves impaired tumor immunosurveillance combined with UV exposure.

causes.cause: High nevus count and atypical (dysplastic) nevi
causes.description: Patients with more than 100 common nevi or 5 or more atypical nevi have markedly elevated melanoma risk. Atypical nevi can be markers of risk and occasionally precursors of new in situ disease.

riskFactors.factor: Fair skin and light pigmentation (Fitzpatrick I-II)
riskFactors.description: Lower constitutive melanin protection raises melanoma risk by 5-10 fold versus darker skin types. Red or blond hair and blue or green eyes amplify the risk through linked genetic pathways.
Lifetime sun exposure correlates with lentigo maligna; intermittent severe sunburns correlate more strongly with superficial spreading melanoma. Outdoor occupations and tropical residence raise risk.
Raises melanoma risk roughly 75% in pooled meta-analyses. The IARC classifies tanning beds as Group 1 human carcinogens.
Five or more atypical nevi confer a 6-10 fold elevated melanoma risk. Patients with familial atypical multiple mole melanoma syndrome require lifelong surveillance.
Two or more first-degree relatives with melanoma raises personal risk 30-70 fold depending on the underlying germline variant (CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1, POT1, MITF E318K).

riskFactors.factor: Personal history of prior melanoma or non-melanoma skin cancer
riskFactors.description: Prior melanoma raises subsequent melanoma risk roughly 9 fold; prior basal or squamous cell carcinoma also raises risk through shared UV exposure.
Lentigo maligna in particular peaks in the eighth and ninth decades, reflecting cumulative UV damage. Total melanoma incidence rises steadily with age.
Solid organ transplant recipients have 3-4 fold elevated melanoma incidence. Calcineurin inhibitors and azathioprine contribute.

affectedDemographics: US: roughly 30,000 new in situ cases annually; 30% of all new melanoma diagnoses (SEER 2024)
Australia: highest national melanoma incidence (49 per 100,000) due to combined fair-skin demographics and UV exposure
Patients with skin of color: in situ melanoma is uncommon but acral lentiginous subtype disproportionately affects them
Lentigo maligna subtype: predominantly older adults aged 70 and above, on chronically sun-damaged head and neck skin
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows the 2019 AAD melanoma guidelines (Swetter JAAD 2019) and the current NCCN melanoma guideline. The pathway begins with clinical examination using the ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter, Evolution) and the ugly duckling sign. Dermoscopy raises diagnostic sensitivity and specificity substantially: superficial spreading in situ shows asymmetric pigment network with abrupt cut-off, irregular dots, and regression structures; lentigo maligna shows asymmetric pigmented follicular openings and rhomboidal structures on chronically sun-damaged skin. Any clinically or dermoscopically suspicious lesion warrants biopsy. The preferred technique is excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm margins of clinically normal skin extending into subcutaneous fat. This allows accurate determination of Breslow thickness, ulceration, mitotic rate, and most importantly distinguishes in situ from invasive disease. Partial biopsies (incisional, punch, shave) are acceptable only on cosmetically sensitive sites such as the face or acral surfaces, with the understanding that they may sample non-representative areas. Histopathologic diagnosis of melanoma in situ requires demonstration of malignant melanocytes confined to the epidermis with characteristic features: nested and single-cell proliferation along the dermo-epidermal junction, pagetoid scatter into upper epidermis, cytologic atypia, and confluent growth. Immunohistochemistry (Melan-A, MITF, SOX10) confirms melanocytic origin and aids margin assessment. Lentigo maligna in particular requires careful margin evaluation because subclinical extension is common — many surgeons employ staged excision (slow Mohs or square procedure) for adequate clearance. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is not indicated for true in situ disease because there is no metastatic potential by definition.

diagnosticTests.test: Total-body skin examination by a dermatologist
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the suspicious lesion in context of the patient's other pigmented lesions; detects the 'ugly duckling' and assesses overall melanoma risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute examination of the entire skin surface including scalp, soles, palms, nail units, and mucosae. The dermatologist may use a dermatoscope (handheld magnifier with polarized light).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Magnified skin examination that improves diagnostic sensitivity and specificity by 30-40% over naked-eye inspection. Identifies asymmetry of pigment, irregular network, regression, and other melanoma-specific features.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless examination using a handheld lighted magnifier pressed gently against the skin. Adds minutes to the visit; no special preparation.

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm clinical margin
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard sampling. Removes the entire lesion with a narrow margin of normal skin, enabling complete histopathologic assessment of depth, margins, and features.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic injection followed by elliptical excision under sterile technique. Most procedures take 15-30 minutes in the office. Sutures removed in 7-14 days depending on site.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms melanocytic origin, distinguishes in situ from invasive disease, and assists margin assessment in challenging cases like lentigo maligna.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by the dermatopathologist on the excised tissue. Results typically available within 5-10 working days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive in vivo imaging that visualizes individual cells at near-histologic resolution. Useful for mapping lentigo maligna margins and guiding biopsy of large facial lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe applied to the skin captures images in real time. Painless; takes 20-30 minutes for a moderate lesion. Available primarily at specialist centers.

diagnosticTests.test: Staged excision (slow Mohs, square procedure) for lentigo maligna
diagnosticTests.purpose: Achieves complete margin assessment for poorly demarcated lentigo maligna on the face. Reduces recurrence to 1-2% versus 6-20% with conventional excision at 5 mm margins.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multiple visits over 1-2 weeks. Each stage involves excising a strip of tissue and waiting for pathology before further excision. Final reconstruction follows clearance.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of melanoma in situ is fundamentally surgical because complete excision both confirms the diagnosis and cures the disease. NCCN Melanoma Guidelines and the 2019 AAD guideline (Swetter JAAD 2019) recommend wide local excision with 5-10 mm margins from the visible edge of the lesion, extending into subcutaneous tissue. Conventional 5 mm margins clear about 80% of lesions histologically; expanding to 9-10 mm clears over 95% for non-lentigo-maligna subtypes. For lentigo maligna specifically, subclinical extension is common and recurrence after standard 5 mm excision reaches 6-20%. Mohs micrographic surgery with melanoma immunostains (MART-1, SOX10) or staged excision techniques such as the slow Mohs or square procedure assess 100% of margins and reduce recurrence to 1-2% (Higgins JAAD 2015). These techniques are preferred for cosmetically sensitive sites and large lentigo maligna of the face. Reconstruction is undertaken after clear margins are documented. Non-surgical alternatives are reserved for patients unfit for or refusing surgery, or for adjuvant treatment of poorly demarcated lentigo maligna. Topical imiquimod 5% cream applied 5 days per week for 12 weeks produces clinical clearance in 70-80% of lentigo maligna, though long-term recurrence rates remain higher than surgery. Radiotherapy (definitive fractionated regimens) achieves local control in 80-90% of lentigo maligna and is an option in elderly or surgically unfit patients. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is not indicated because in situ disease lacks metastatic potential by definition. After treatment, surveillance is essential: dermatology follow-up every 6 months for 5 years, then annually, plus total-body skin examination given a 9-fold elevated risk of subsequent melanoma. Patient education on photoprotection, monthly self-skin examination, and risk-factor modification is integral.

medicalTreatments.name: Imiquimod 5% cream (topical)
medicalTreatments.description: Toll-like receptor 7 agonist that stimulates local immune response against melanoma cells. Applied 5 days per week for 12 weeks, with clinical and histologic monitoring. Reserved for patients unfit for surgery, refusing surgery, or as adjuvant after positive Mohs margins in lentigo maligna.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial clinical clearance in 70-80% of lentigo maligna; long-term recurrence rates 20-30% at 5 years — inferior to complete surgical excision.

medicalTreatments.name: Radiotherapy (definitive, fractionated; typically 45-50 Gy in 25 fractions or comparable hypofractionated schedule)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as definitive therapy when surgery is contraindicated or refused, or as adjuvant after incomplete excision of lentigo maligna. Cosmetic outcome can be excellent on the face.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Local control in 80-90% of lentigo maligna at 5 years; relatively few prospective comparative data versus Mohs.

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy (specialist use only)
medicalTreatments.description: Rarely used and not first-line. Destroys tissue without histologic margin assessment, so recurrence is harder to detect. Reserved for very superficial lesions in patients unsuitable for other modalities.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable; not recommended as standard care.

surgicalOptions.name: Wide local excision (5-10 mm margins)
surgicalOptions.description: First-line treatment for most melanoma in situ. Excision is performed under local anesthesia for small lesions or general anesthesia for large or anatomically complex sites. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is not indicated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Histologic clearance in over 95% with 9-10 mm margins for non-lentigo-maligna subtypes; recurrence under 5%.

surgicalOptions.name: Mohs micrographic surgery with melanoma immunostains
surgicalOptions.description: Tissue-sparing technique that maps 100% of the surgical margin using frozen or paraffin sections with melanocytic immunostains (MART-1, SOX10). Standard of care for lentigo maligna and cosmetically sensitive sites.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Local recurrence 1-2% at 5 years for lentigo maligna versus 6-20% with conventional excision (Higgins JAAD 2015).

surgicalOptions.name: Staged excision (slow Mohs or square procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: Alternative to traditional Mohs that uses permanent (paraffin-embedded) sections for more reliable melanocyte visualization. Multiple visits over 1-2 weeks with final reconstruction once margins clear.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Local recurrence 2-5% at 5 years; comparable to MART-1 Mohs.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist with melanoma expertise should manage diagnosis and surgical planning. Mohs surgeons or surgical oncologists are involved for cosmetically challenging or anatomically complex lentigo maligna of the face. Patients with familial melanoma, multiple primary melanomas, or strong family history warrant genetic counseling for CDKN2A and other germline variants. Multidisciplinary tumor boards review difficult lentigo maligna cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board-certified dermatologists or surgical oncologists with melanoma-specific volume, on-site dermoscopy, access to Mohs surgery with melanoma immunostains for facial lesions, and integration with dermatopathology. Tertiary cancer centers offer staged excision, reflectance confocal microscopy, and clinical trials for advanced lentigo maligna.
preventionStrategies: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily to sun-exposed skin and reapply every 2 hours during outdoor activity
Avoid tanning beds entirely — any use before age 35 raises melanoma risk by roughly 75%
Wear photoprotective clothing, wide-brim hats, and UV-blocking sunglasses during peak UV hours (10 AM to 4 PM)
Seek shade during peak UV hours; UV index applications guide planning
Perform monthly self-skin examination with attention to new or changing lesions; partners can help check the back and scalp
Annual full-body dermatology examination for adults with risk factors (fair skin, atypical nevi, family history, prior skin cancer)
lifestyleModifications: Stop indoor tanning and discourage it among family members and adolescents
Adopt sun-safe outdoor habits even in winter and at altitude
Track changing lesions with smartphone photographs at fixed lighting to detect evolution
Schedule lifelong dermatology follow-up after any melanoma diagnosis — 6-monthly for 5 years, then annually
Educate household members on melanoma warning signs; familial clustering exists
Continue vitamin D supplementation if dietary intake is low — sun avoidance can reduce cutaneous vitamin D synthesis

recommended: Vegetables and fruit rich in carotenoids and polyphenols for antioxidant support
Oily fish for omega-3 fatty acids — modest observational evidence of skin cancer risk reduction
Adequate vitamin D from diet or supplements (800-2000 IU daily) to compensate for sun avoidance
Green tea — observational studies suggest a protective association though evidence is limited
Whole grains and lean protein for general dermatologic health
avoid: Excess alcohol — observational data link heavy intake to higher melanoma risk
Tobacco smoking — worsens wound healing and overall cancer risk
Diets very low in vitamin D and antioxidants if sun avoidance is strict
Unproven supplements marketed for melanoma prevention without evidence
Tanning supplements (e.g., melanotan injections) — unlicensed, unsafe, and increase melanoma risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular outdoor exercise is encouraged with sun-safe planning — protective clothing, sunscreen, and hat in daylight hours. Indoor cardio and resistance training are appropriate during peak UV periods. No exercise restrictions follow melanoma in situ excision once the wound has healed (usually 2-3 weeks).
dailyManagement: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen daily to sun-exposed areas regardless of weather
Self-examine skin monthly with attention to new lesions and changes in existing moles
Attend scheduled dermatology surveillance every 6 months for the first 5 years
Monitor surgical scar for any pigmented or thickened recurrence — report promptly
Educate first-degree relatives about melanoma warning signs and risk
prognosis: Prognosis after complete excision of true melanoma in situ is excellent. Five-year disease-specific survival exceeds 99% because in situ lesions have no biological access to lymphatics or blood vessels. The dominant residual risks are local recurrence (1-2% with Mohs or staged excision for lentigo maligna; 6-20% with conventional 5 mm excision) and the development of a second primary melanoma elsewhere on the skin (roughly 9-fold elevated lifetime risk versus the general population). Recurrent lentigo maligna can progress to lentigo maligna melanoma (invasive disease) if neglected — long-term dermatology surveillance is therefore essential. Adverse factors include incomplete excision margins, large lentigo maligna (>3 cm), and the lentigo maligna subtype on cosmetically constrained sites such as the eyelid or nasal ala. Favorable factors include early detection, small lesion size, clear margins documented with comprehensive margin assessment, and patient adherence to surveillance and photoprotection.
recoveryTimeline: Surgical wounds typically heal in 2-3 weeks. Sutures are removed in 7-14 days depending on site and tension. Sun protection of the scar is essential for 6-12 months. Lentigo maligna treated with staged excision or Mohs may involve 1-2 weeks of multiple visits before final reconstruction; complex reconstructions on the face may take additional weeks of healing. Dermatology surveillance begins at 3-6 months post-procedure and continues every 6 months for the first 5 years, then annually.
complications: Local recurrence at the excision site — most common in lentigo maligna with conventional margins
Progression of recurrent in situ disease to invasive (Breslow-positive) melanoma if surveillance lapses
Cosmetic and functional disfigurement of large facial excisions — important consideration on the eyelid, lip, or nose
Development of a second primary melanoma elsewhere — 9-fold elevated lifetime risk
Surgical complications including infection, dehiscence, and unsatisfactory scarring — uncommon but possible
Psychological impact of cancer diagnosis and lifelong surveillance — addressable with counseling and patient support groups

supportResources.name: Melanoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization providing education, advocacy, and access to clinical trials.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Melanoma
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information from the body that publishes the AAD melanoma guideline.

supportResources.name: Skin Cancer Foundation
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information including prevention, detection, and treatment of melanoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Basal Cell Carcinoma (Pigmented)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pigmented basal cell carcinoma can mimic melanoma in situ clinically and dermoscopically. Histopathology distinguishes them: BCC shows basaloid nests in the dermis with peripheral palisading, while melanoma in situ shows malignant melanocytic proliferation confined to the epidermis. BCC stains for BerEP4; melanoma stains for Melan-A, MITF, SOX10.

confusedWithConditions.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma In Situ (Bowen Disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SCC in situ presents as an erythematous scaly plaque rather than a pigmented macule, on chronically sun-exposed skin. Histopathology shows keratinocytic atypia confined to the epidermis with intact basement membrane. Distinguished from melanoma in situ by immunohistochemistry (cytokeratin positive, Melan-A negative).

confusedWithConditions.name: Actinic Keratosis (Pigmented)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pigmented actinic keratosis is a sun-induced pre-malignant keratinocytic lesion on chronically sun-damaged skin. It is rough or scaly to touch, lacks the asymmetric pigment pattern of lentigo maligna on dermoscopy, and shows keratinocytic dysplasia without melanocytic atypia on biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carcinoma In Situ (Non-Melanoma)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Carcinoma in situ is a generic term for malignant epithelial proliferation confined to the epithelium of various organs. Melanoma in situ is specifically melanocytic and stains for Melan-A, MITF, and SOX10. Both share the in situ concept of no basement membrane breach.

relatedConditions.name: Basal Cell Carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares UV exposure as the dominant risk factor; patients with melanoma in situ have elevated lifetime risk of basal cell carcinoma.

relatedConditions.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both UV-driven; field cancerization on sun-damaged skin produces multiple keratinocytic and melanocytic neoplasms in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Carcinoma In Situ
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the concept of malignant proliferation confined to the epithelium without basement membrane invasion.

relatedConditions.name: Kaposi Sarcoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Another pigmented skin malignancy in the differential of new pigmented lesions, especially in immunosuppressed patients.

relatedConditions.name: Actinic Keratosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Marker of chronic UV exposure; patients with multiple actinic keratoses are at elevated risk for lentigo maligna and other UV-driven skin cancers.

faqs.question: What is melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Melanoma in situ is the earliest stage of melanoma. Malignant melanocytes are confined entirely to the epidermis, the outermost layer of skin, without invading the dermis or having access to lymphatics. It is classified as Stage 0 by the AJCC and has a 5-year disease-specific survival exceeding 99% after complete excision.

faqs.question: Is melanoma in situ cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Melanoma in situ is a true cancer because the cells are malignant — they show cytologic atypia and proliferate without normal regulation. However, because the cells are confined to the epidermis above the basement membrane, they have no biological access to blood vessels or lymphatics and cannot metastasize while in situ.

faqs.question: Can melanoma in situ become invasive melanoma?
faqs.answer: Yes, if untreated or incompletely excised. Untreated melanoma in situ, particularly lentigo maligna, can progress to invasive melanoma over years. Complete surgical excision essentially eliminates this risk, which is why early diagnosis and treatment are so important.

faqs.question: How is melanoma in situ treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is wide local excision with 5-10 mm margins of clinically normal skin. For lentigo maligna and cosmetically sensitive sites, Mohs micrographic surgery with melanoma immunostains or staged excision is preferred to reduce recurrence. Topical imiquimod or radiotherapy are alternatives for patients unfit for surgery.

faqs.question: What margins are recommended for melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: NCCN guidelines recommend 5-10 mm margins from the visible edge for melanoma in situ. Lentigo maligna often requires wider margins or comprehensive margin assessment (Mohs or staged excision) because subclinical extension is common. Margins are measured from the clinical edge to the histologically clear margin.

faqs.question: Does melanoma in situ require a sentinel lymph node biopsy?
faqs.answer: No. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is not indicated for true melanoma in situ because in situ disease lacks access to lymphatics and has no metastatic potential by definition. If invasive components are found on the final excision specimen, the staging is reclassified and sentinel node biopsy is considered.

faqs.question: What is lentigo maligna?
faqs.answer: Lentigo maligna is a subtype of melanoma in situ that arises on chronically sun-damaged skin, classically the head and neck of older adults. It presents as a slowly enlarging irregularly pigmented macule. Subclinical extension is common, so wider margins or comprehensive margin assessment (Mohs, staged excision) is preferred.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Five-year disease-specific survival exceeds 99% after complete excision. Long-term survival is essentially equivalent to the general population. Patients remain at elevated risk for new primary melanomas elsewhere on the skin (roughly 9-fold lifetime risk) and require lifelong dermatology surveillance.

faqs.question: Can melanoma in situ come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Local recurrence is uncommon with adequate excision: 1-2% after Mohs or staged excision for lentigo maligna and 6-20% with conventional 5 mm margins for lentigo maligna. Non-lentigo-maligna in situ subtypes recur in under 5% after 9-10 mm margins. Recurrence warrants further excision and pathologic review.

faqs.question: Is melanoma in situ caused by sun exposure?
faqs.answer: Most cases are linked to ultraviolet exposure. Lentigo maligna correlates with cumulative chronic sun exposure; superficial spreading in situ correlates with intermittent severe sunburns, especially in childhood. Tanning bed use before age 35 raises melanoma risk by roughly 75%. Genetic susceptibility (CDKN2A, MC1R) modifies individual risk.

faqs.question: How can I tell a mole from melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Use the ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation (two or more shades), Diameter typically over 6 mm, and Evolution (any recent change in size, shape, color, or symptoms). The ugly duckling sign — a lesion that looks different from your other moles — is also useful. Any suspicious lesion warrants dermatology assessment.

faqs.question: Does melanoma in situ run in families?
faqs.answer: Familial melanoma accounts for roughly 5-10% of cases. Two or more first-degree relatives with melanoma, multiple primary melanomas in one individual, or melanoma plus pancreatic cancer in a family raises suspicion for germline variants like CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1, POT1, or MITF E318K. Genetic counseling is recommended in such families.

faqs.question: What is Mohs surgery for melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Mohs micrographic surgery removes melanoma in situ in thin layers and examines 100% of the surgical margin under the microscope before further excision. Used with melanoma immunostains (MART-1, SOX10), it reduces local recurrence to 1-2% for lentigo maligna. It is preferred for facial lesions where margin assessment and tissue conservation matter.

faqs.question: Can I use imiquimod cream instead of surgery for melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Imiquimod 5% cream applied 5 days per week for 12 weeks produces clinical clearance in 70-80% of lentigo maligna. It is an alternative for patients unfit for or refusing surgery, or as adjuvant after positive Mohs margins. Long-term recurrence rates remain higher than surgery, so it is not first-line for most patients.

faqs.question: How often should I see a dermatologist after melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: After excision, dermatology follow-up is typically every 6 months for the first 5 years, then annually. Total-body skin examinations search for new primary melanomas and inspect the scar for local recurrence. Patients with multiple risk factors or strong family history may benefit from more frequent surveillance.

faqs.question: Can melanoma in situ appear under a nail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acral lentiginous melanoma in situ can present as a new pigmented streak under a fingernail or toenail (longitudinal melanonychia). A streak wider than 3 mm, irregular pigmentation, or extension onto the surrounding nail fold skin (Hutchinson sign) warrants nail unit biopsy. This subtype is more common in people of color.

faqs.question: Does melanoma in situ affect people with dark skin?
faqs.answer: Melanoma in situ is less common in people with darker skin but does occur. The acral lentiginous subtype on palms, soles, and nail units is disproportionately represented in people of color and is often diagnosed later. Total-body skin examination including acral surfaces and nails is recommended for all skin types.

faqs.question: What is the cost of melanoma in situ treatment?
faqs.answer: Excisional biopsy and wide local excision range from USD 300 to 2,000 in the US depending on lesion size and reconstruction. Mohs surgery for lentigo maligna is typically USD 1,500-4,000. Most procedures are covered by insurance because melanoma is a malignancy. Costs in other markets vary widely.

faqs.question: Should I have my children screened for melanoma?
faqs.answer: Routine pediatric melanoma screening is not recommended, but children of patients with familial melanoma, multiple primary melanomas, or known germline variants warrant dermatology surveillance starting in adolescence. Sun-protective habits established in childhood are the most important prevention strategy.

faqs.question: Is sunscreen enough to prevent melanoma in situ?
faqs.answer: Sunscreen reduces but does not eliminate melanoma risk. Broad-spectrum SPF 30+ applied correctly and reapplied every 2 hours is part of comprehensive photoprotection that also includes UV-protective clothing, hats, sunglasses, shade during peak hours, avoiding tanning beds, and self-skin examinations.

metaTitle: Melanoma In Situ: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Melanoma in situ: stage 0 melanoma confined to epidermis. Symptoms, diagnosis, Mohs surgery, margins, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.

sources.title: Swetter SM, Tsao H, Bichakjian CK, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of primary cutaneous melanoma. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology. 2019;80(1):208-250.
Higgins HW 2nd, Lee KC, Galan A, Leffell DJ. Melanoma in situ: Part II. Histopathology, treatment, and clinical management. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology. 2015;73(2):193-203.
